name: Towel Behind
purpose: Groove ball-first contact and move the swing's low point forward.
setup: Fold a towel and place it one grip-length behind the ball on the target line.
steps: Make half-swings, clipping the ball without touching the towel.
Build to fuller swings as contact stays clean.
Move the towel closer to the ball as you succeed.
goal: 7 of 10 clean strikes without touching the towel.
visualConcept: Top-down lane showing ball, target line, and a shaded towel zone behind the ball; green clip point ahead of the ball. Club low point arrives after the ball.
rationale: Low-point control is the core amateur ball-striking skill; the towel gives instant tactile feedback without a technical swing thought.

name: Toe-Heel Gate
purpose: Train center-face strike quality and remove toe/heel mishits.
setup: Push two tees into the turf just wider than the clubhead, one at the toe and one at the heel, with the ball centered between them.
steps: Swing through the ball without knocking either tee.
Start with a wide gate for confidence.
Narrow the gate as your center contact improves.
goal: 5 clean passes in a row without disturbing a tee.
visualConcept: Head-on clubface with two vertical gateposts at toe and heel; a strike heatmap dot lands in the center. Knocking a post flashes red.
rationale: Center-face contact is the most reliable distance and accuracy gain for amateurs; the gate gives binary feedback without a technical cue.

name: Feet-Together Balance
purpose: Expose and smooth out rushed tempo by removing the base of support.
setup: Stand with your feet touching, a mid-iron in hand, ball teed low, swinging at 60 to 70 percent effort.
steps: Make controlled swings with feet together.
Hold a balanced finish after each shot.
If you lose balance, ease the effort until the swing stays centered.
goal: 8 of 10 swings with a held, balanced finish and no stumble.
visualConcept: Figure with a narrow base and a balance meter beside it that tips when the swing overpowers the stance.
rationale: Removing the base forces rhythm over muscle and is a standard self-correcting tempo diagnostic — no metronome or counting required.

name: Driver Gate
purpose: Train a neutral-to-square path and center strike off the tee.
setup: Tee the ball and place an alignment rod about three to four inches outside the ball, parallel to the target line, as an outer boundary.
steps: Swing so the club approaches the ball without hitting the rod.
Check the rod stays in place after each swing.
Confirm the ball starts on your intended line.
goal: 7 of 10 clean swings with the ball starting on line.
visualConcept: Top-down tee box showing ball, target line, and the outer rod boundary; a green approach corridor the club must travel down.
rationale: For most recreational golfers a neutral path plus square face beats chasing a shaped shot; the corridor visual trains it without a swing-fault label.

name: Three-and-Switch
purpose: Hold the same tempo across the driver and wedge, the two clubs amateurs most often rush and decelerate.
setup: Have a driver and a wedge ready on the range.
steps: Hit 3 driver swings at a smooth tempo.
Immediately hit 3 wedge swings at the same tempo.
Alternate back and forth for 5 rounds.
goal: Both clubs feel identical in rhythm across a full round of the drill.
visualConcept: Split screen with two clubs sharing one tempo waveform (roughly 3:1 back-to-through) pulsing across both.
rationale: Tempo should stay constant across clubs on full swings; alternating exposes when the driver speeds up relative to the wedge.

name: Carry Ladder
purpose: Build a reliable carry-distance map for partial wedge shots.
setup: Pick three targets (for example 30, 50, and 70 yards) or use three swing-length stations (half, three-quarter, full) with one wedge.
steps: Hit a cluster of shots to each distance, noting your stock swing feel for each.
Repeat until each distance has a repeatable feel.
Randomize the called distance to test recall.
goal: Land 3 of 5 in each target zone, then survive a randomized round.
visualConcept: Range fan with three concentric carry bands; shot dots cluster into each. Randomized mode reshuffles the called distance.
rationale: Distance control from wedge range is a top strokes-gained lever; the ladder is the canonical method and the random finish forces course transfer.

name: Trajectory Three-Ways
purpose: Produce low, medium, and high wedge flights on demand with a single club.
setup: Take one wedge to one distance of about 40 yards from an open lie.
steps: Hit the target with a low runner.
Hit the same target with a standard flight.
Hit it again with a high, soft flight, varying ball position and face.
goal: Three distinctly different flights all finishing near the target.
visualConcept: One target with three overlaid trajectory arcs (low, mid, high) in different shades.
rationale: Loft and trajectory management with one club is a core, credible short-game skill that builds real on-course versatility.

name: Landing-Spot Towel
purpose: Shift focus from the hole to a chosen landing spot, the actual controllable variable in chipping.
setup: Lay a towel as the landing target 3 to 15 feet out from a standard chip lie.
steps: Chip to land the ball on the towel, ignoring where it finishes at first.
Once you can land it reliably, read how far it rolls out.
Adjust club or landing spot to finish near the hole.
cue: Land it on the towel, let it roll.
goal: 5 of 10 chips land on the towel.
visualConcept: Green with a highlighted landing rectangle plus a dotted roll-out path continuing to the hole.
rationale: Landing-spot control is the primary chipping skill taught by top coaches; it separates the carry you control from the rollout you predict.

name: Roll-Out Ratios
purpose: Learn each club's carry-to-roll ratio so club choice around the green becomes deliberate.
setup: Use the same landing spot and the same chip, rotating through a pitching wedge, 9-iron, 8-iron, and sand wedge.
steps: Land each club on the same spot.
Watch how far each one rolls out.
Log the pattern for each club.
goal: Correctly predict which club stops closest before hitting it.
visualConcept: One landing spot with four color-coded roll-out lines of different lengths fanning to different finish points.
rationale: Club-versus-rollout awareness is a proven, decision-focused chipping concept that trains why to pick a club, not just how to swing it.

name: Lead-Hand Only
purpose: Kill the scoop and flip that cause chunked and skulled chips.
setup: Take a short chip with only your lead hand on the club, trail hand off or lightly supporting.
steps: Chip short ones keeping the hand ahead of the clubhead through impact.
Build a feel for the handle leading the strike.
Add the trail hand back while keeping the same feel.
goal: 8 of 10 clean contacts one-handed.
visualConcept: Impact freeze-frame showing the handle ahead of the clubhead; a red 'flip' ghost shows the failure mode.
rationale: Preventing the wrist flip is the most-cited chipping contact fix; lead-hand-only enforces it physically instead of as a swing thought.

name: Splash Line
purpose: Train a consistent sand-entry point and a shallow splash that uses the bounce instead of digging.
setup: Draw a line in the sand perpendicular to the target, with no ball to start.
steps: Splash the sand entering on the line with an open face and a shallow path.
Repeat until entry is consistent.
Add a ball on the line once the splash is reliable.
goal: 8 of 10 entries on the line with a shallow splash.
visualConcept: Sand cross-section showing a shallow club path skimming under a banknote-sized splash zone, versus a red steep-dig path.
rationale: Consistent entry and a shallow splash using the bounce is the foundational sand skill across every coaching source.

name: Bunker Ladder
purpose: Control sand-shot distance through follow-through length rather than swing violence.
setup: Use the same lie with targets set progressively farther onto the green.
steps: Keep the same entry and splash for every shot.
Vary only the follow-through: short, half, then full finish.
Make each ball carry past the previous one.
goal: Land each successive ball past the previous, 4 in a row.
visualConcept: Bunker with a fan of landing bands and a follow-through length meter tied to carry distance.
rationale: Distance-by-follow-through with a constant splash is the standard pro method for sand distance control.

name: Start-Line Gate
purpose: Roll the ball on the intended start line with a centered strike.
setup: Place two tees just wider than the ball about 12 inches ahead on a straight putt, with the hole about 4 feet away.
steps: Putt through the gate without clipping a tee.
Make 3 in a row.
Progress to 5 in a row.
cue: Through the gate, into the hole.
goal: 5 clean makes in a row.
visualConcept: Top-down straight putt with a narrow tee gate on the line to the cup; clipping a tee flashes red.
rationale: The gate trains start line and center-face contact together and is the most trusted, most visual putting drill in the game.

name: Distance Ladder
purpose: Build lag feel and eliminate three-putts.
setup: Set tees or markers as target zones at increasing distances, for example every few feet from 15 to 40 feet.
steps: Roll putts to stop inside each successive zone, working outward.
Then work back inward.
Randomize the called zone to finish.
cue: Roll it to die in the zone.
goal: Land consecutive putts in successive zones without overshooting.
visualConcept: Green with stacked distance bands like rungs; the ball must stop inside each rung.
rationale: Speed control is the dominant three-putt-avoidance skill; the ladder trains feel across a range of lengths.

name: Clock Circle
purpose: Convert makeable short putts under mild pressure.
setup: Place balls at the 3, 6, 9, and 12 o'clock positions around the hole, about 3 feet from the cup.
steps: Hole all four in a row.
A miss restarts the circle.
Extend the distance as you succeed.
goal: Clear the full circle without a miss, then repeat from 4 feet.
visualConcept: Overhead hole with four ball positions on a clock ring; the ring lights up as each is holed.
rationale: Short-putt conversion under a make-in-a-row requirement builds confidence and pressure tolerance where rounds are saved.

name: Par 18
purpose: Practice the full up-and-down process under scorekeeping pressure.
setup: Choose 9 different chip locations around a green; each is a par-2 (chip plus putt).
steps: Chip and putt out each location.
Total your strokes against a par of 18.
Log the score and try to beat it next time.
cue: Every chip has a putt attached.
goal: Score 18 or better over the 9 locations.
visualConcept: Green map with 9 numbered stations ringing it and a running scorecard.
rationale: A tour-favorite up-and-down game that gives instant weakness feedback and is inherently varied, driving practice into real scoring.

name: Nine-Shot Range
purpose: Vary club, target, and flight every shot to mimic on-course demand.
setup: Have your full bag on the range.
steps: On every ball, choose a new club and a new target.
Run your full pre-shot routine as if playing 18 holes.
Never hit two consecutive shots alike.
cue: New shot, new routine, every time.
goal: Play a full simulated front nine without repeating a shot.
visualConcept: Range with rotating target flags and a shuffling club/target prompt card each rep.
rationale: Random practice with a full pre-shot routine is the best-supported method for course transfer in the motor-learning literature.

name: Two-Ball Takeaway
purpose: Smooth a rushed takeaway.
setup: Place two balls on the target line, one about 3 inches behind the other, and set up to the front ball.
steps: On the backswing, knock the rear ball away.
Swing through and strike the front ball cleanly.
Slow the takeaway just enough to reach the rear ball first.
goal: Clean front-ball strike 7 of 10.
visualConcept: Two balls inline with a low, smooth takeaway animation brushing the rear one before striking the front.
rationale: Slowing the takeaway smooths path and tempo without a mechanical thought; optional depth alongside the primary tempo drills.

name: Continuous Swings
purpose: Remove stop-start rhythm and let the swing carry itself.
setup: Line up 3 to 4 balls in a row.
steps: Strike each ball without pausing between them.
Keep the club always moving.
Hold the final finish after the last ball.
goal: All balls struck with unbroken rhythm.
visualConcept: Continuous looping swing waveform flowing across multiple balls.
rationale: Trains flowing tempo and sequencing; optional depth for players who want a second continuous-motion tempo option.

name: Circle-Your-Foot
purpose: Learn the entry point and splash size in greenside sand.
setup: Draw a foot-sized circle in the sand with the ball in the center.
steps: Enter the sand behind the ball at the back of the circle.
Splash the whole circle out, exiting in front.
Accelerate through so the ball rides out on the sand.
goal: Consistently remove the full circle with the ball riding out.
visualConcept: Sand with a highlighted oval 'island' lifting out as one piece.
rationale: Teaches splash size and shallow entry visually; optional depth that overlaps Splash Line, so use whichever visual you prefer.
